title: SEGMENT 6 — Indications: what HSCT is actually used for

lines.text: SEGMENT 6 — Indications: what HSCT is actually used for
The list of conditions for which hematopoietic stem cell transplant is the standard of care, or a major treatment option, is long, but it falls into a few clean categories.
Let me walk through them, because each one teaches you something about why the procedure works the way it does.
Category one: the acute leukemias.
These are the cancers that started the field and remain its largest single indication.
Acute myeloid leukemia, AML, is a cancer of myeloid lineage progenitors in the bone marrow.
Acute lymphoblastic leukemia, ALL, is the analogous cancer of lymphoid progenitors, more common in children.
Both diseases are characterized by the accumulation of immature, non-functional white cell precursors that crowd out normal hematopoiesis.
Untreated, both are rapidly fatal.
With standard chemotherapy, many patients achieve initial remission, but for high-risk subtypes or for patients who relapse, the only curative option is allogeneic HSCT.
The transplant works by two mechanisms: the high-dose conditioning chemo kills residual leukemia, and the donor immune system mops up whatever survives via the graft-versus-tumor effect.
AML and ALL together account for a large fraction of all allogeneic transplants performed worldwide.
Category two: the chronic leukemias and the myeloproliferative neoplasms.
Chronic myeloid leukemia, CML, was historically a major transplant indication — driven by a defining genetic abnormality, the Philadelphia chromosome, which creates a fusion gene called BCR-ABL.
For decades, allogeneic HSCT was the only curative therapy for CML.

Then, in 2001, a small-molecule drug called imatinib (Gleevec) was approved that specifically inhibited the BCR-ABL fusion protein, and it turned CML from a transplant disease into a pill-managed chronic condition for most patients.
This is one of the great success stories of targeted therapy, and it also illustrates how the indications for transplant evolve.
CML transplants are now rare and reserved for patients whose disease has become resistant to multiple tyrosine kinase inhibitors.
The other myeloproliferative diseases — myelofibrosis, polycythemia vera, essential thrombocythemia in advanced phases — still occasionally come to transplant when other options are exhausted.
Chronic lymphocytic leukemia, CLL, was historically transplanted in younger, fit patients, but with the rise of BTK inhibitors and now CAR-T, transplant for CLL has also become uncommon.
Category three: the lymphomas and myeloma.
Non-Hodgkin lymphomas and Hodgkin lymphoma, when they relapse after initial chemotherapy, are typically treated with autologous HSCT — the high-dose chemo rescue paradigm.
The lymphoma cells are killed by intensified chemo, and the patient's own stem cells, collected beforehand, are re-infused to rebuild the marrow.
Multiple myeloma, a malignancy of plasma cells, is the single largest indication for autologous HSCT worldwide.
Patients typically receive induction chemotherapy, undergo autologous transplant with high-dose melphalan, and then continue on maintenance therapy.
The transplant doesn't cure myeloma — it delays relapse — but it remains a key part of the standard treatment paradigm for fit patients.
There are tens of thousands of autologous transplants done annually for myeloma globally.
Category four: severe aplastic anemia.
This is a non-malignant condition where the bone marrow simply fails — typically due to an autoimmune attack on the patient's own HSCs.
The marrow becomes empty, and the patient becomes pancytopenic — low in red cells, white cells, and platelets.
Without treatment, severe aplastic anemia is fatal.
Allogeneic HSCT, especially from a matched sibling, is curative in 80 to 90 percent of patients, and it is the standard of care for young patients with a matched donor.
For older patients or those without a donor, the alternative is immunosuppression with antithymocyte globulin plus cyclosporine, but transplant is more durable.
Category five: the inherited immune deficiencies.
Severe combined immunodeficiency, SCID — also called "bubble boy disease" — is a group of genetic diseases in which infants are born essentially without a functional immune system.
Untreated, they die in the first year of life from opportunistic infections.
Allogeneic HSCT, especially when done in the first three months of life with an HLA-matched sibling, is curative in over 90 percent of cases.
SCID was one of the very first diseases for which bone marrow transplant was established as a cure, and it remains a paradigm.
Other primary immunodeficiencies — Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome, chronic granulomatous disease, hyper-IgM syndrome — are also treated with allogeneic HSCT in many cases.
Category six: the inherited blood disorders.
This is the category that is currently being transformed by gene therapy.

Sickle cell disease, caused by a single-base mutation in the beta-globin gene producing abnormal hemoglobin that causes red cells to deform and cause vaso-occlusive crises, has been curable by allogeneic HSCT from a matched sibling for decades — with cure rates around 85 percent, but with substantial GVHD and mortality risk, and with the access constraint that only about 20 percent of patients have a matched sibling.
Beta-thalassemia, caused by mutations that reduce beta-globin production and lead to severe anemia requiring lifelong transfusions, is also curable by allogeneic HSCT.
Both diseases, until very recently, had transplant as their only definitive cure.
We'll spend a whole segment on this, because the new gene therapies — Casgevy, Zynteglo, Lyfgenia — are reshaping the treatment landscape for these conditions.
Category seven: the inherited metabolic and storage diseases.

Some rare inherited diseases — adrenoleukodystrophy, certain mucopolysaccharidoses, Krabbe disease — are treated with allogeneic HSCT or with autologous gene therapy (Skysona for adrenoleukodystrophy is an example) because donor-derived myeloid cells can cross into tissues and deliver missing enzymes to deficient cells, partially reversing the metabolic defect.
This use of transplant as a kind of long-term enzyme delivery system is one of the more elegant applications of the procedure.
Category eight, emerging: autoimmune diseases.

This is newer and still mostly experimental, but for the most severe cases of systemic sclerosis, multiple sclerosis, Crohn's disease, and lupus, autologous HSCT after immunoablative conditioning is being used to "reset" the immune system.
The idea is to wipe out the autoreactive immune cells and let the marrow rebuild a more naive, possibly tolerant immune system.
Results have been mixed but encouraging in selected high-risk patients.
So that's the indication map.
Leukemias and lymphomas, the cancers.
Aplastic anemia and SCID, the bone marrow failures and inherited immunodeficiencies.
Sickle cell and thalassemia, the inherited red cell disorders.
Storage diseases, where transplant becomes enzyme delivery.
Autoimmune disease, the experimental frontier.
In every case, the procedure follows the same skeleton — wipe out the existing marrow, infuse new stem cells, wait for engraftment — but the purpose changes.
Sometimes you're replacing cancerous cells.
Sometimes you're delivering a missing gene.
Sometimes you're resetting an autoimmune system.
The chassis is the same.
The payload is what defines the treatment.
Now we have to talk about the chassis — specifically, the most dangerous part of it — which is conditioning.
